content.problemStatement:
"What do you know about happiness?" — Yoda

Chef is happy only if three conditions hold:


Chef finished cooking a delicious meal
Chef got AC for a programming problem with an almost correct code
Chef got a new problem with a sequence of integers
Today, all three conditions are satisfied. Chef would like you to feel his happiness and provide him with a solution for this new problem with a sequence of integers. The problem is as follows.

You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. You need to determine if it is possible to choose two indices iii and jjj such that Ai≠AjA_i \neq A_jAi​=Aj​, but AAiA_{A_i}AAi​​ = AAjA_{A_j}AAj​​. (If it was possible, Chef would be truly happy.)
constraints: 1 ≤ Ai ≤ N for each valid i
The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string "Truly Happy" if it is possible to choose required indices or "Poor Chef" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.output:
Truly Happy
Poor Chef
Poor Chef
Truly Happy
sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example case 1: Chef is truly happy because A3 = A1 and A3 != A1. Example case 2: There is no pair of indices which would make Chef truly happy. For instance, A3 = A4, but A3 = A4.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a hashing approach. We will create a hashmap to store the indices of the elements we have seen so far. Then, for each element, we will check if the element at the index equal to the current element has been seen before. If yes, and the current element is not equal to the element at the index equal to the current element, we can return "Truly Happy". If we have checked all elements and haven't found such a pair, we can return "Poor Chef".
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum number of elements in the array. This is because we are iterating over the array for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the maximum number of elements in the array. This is because we are using a hashmap to store the indices of the elements we have seen so far.
